title: The lungs & gas exchange — breathing made sense
examTip: AQA almost always asks you to explain how alveoli are adapted for gas exchange — you need to name each adaptation AND explain the function it serves. Breathing mechanism questions come up regularly too.
introTips: This lesson covers the structure of the lungs and alveoli, and why each feature makes gas exchange faster.
You'll be able to explain the breathing mechanism — what muscles contract and why that moves air in or out.
AQA often gives you a table of inhaled vs exhaled air and asks you to explain the differences — we'll make sure you can do that confidently.
intro: Right now, without thinking about it, you're exchanging oxygen and carbon dioxide with the air around you — about 20,000 times today. The reason it happens fast enough to keep you alive is that your lungs are exquisitely engineered for one job: getting gases across a barrier as quickly as possible. If you flattened out all the tiny air sacs in your lungs, they'd cover roughly the same area as a tennis court. That enormous surface, packed into your chest, is what makes it work.

diagrams.title: The Lungs — Alveoli and Gas Exchange

keyPoints.heading: Alveoli — why they're perfect for gas exchange
keyPoints.content: Your lungs contain about 300 million tiny air sacs called alveoli (singular: alveolus). Each one is a bubble of air surrounded by a mesh of blood capillaries. Four features make them incredibly efficient at gas exchange. First, there are so many of them that they give an enormous surface area — more contact between air and blood means faster diffusion. Second, the walls are only one cell thick — gases don't have far to travel. Third, the inner surface is moist — gases dissolve before crossing. Fourth, a rich supply of blood capillaries keeps concentration gradients steep: blood arriving has low O₂ and high CO₂, so diffusion always runs in the right direction.
writeBullets: Alveoli: tiny air sacs in the lungs — ~300 million of them
Large surface area → more space for diffusion to occur
Walls one cell thick → short diffusion distance, faster exchange
Moist lining → gases dissolve and cross more easily
Rich blood supply → maintains steep concentration gradient for O₂ and CO₂

keyTerms.def: A tiny air sac in the lungs with thin, moist walls and a rich blood supply; the site of gas exchange
The difference in concentration of a substance across a membrane — the steeper it is, the faster diffusion occurs
The total area available for exchange; larger surface area means faster overall diffusion

keyPoints.memTip: Use SLIM to remember alveoli adaptations: Surface area (large), Lining (moist), Inside distance (thin walls — one cell thick), Maintained gradient (rich blood supply). Say 'SLIM alveoli' before every past paper question on this.

keyPoints.heading: Breathing in and out — the mechanism
keyPoints.content: Breathing isn't passive — it takes muscular work. When you breathe in (inspiration): your intercostal muscles (between the ribs) contract and pull the ribcage up and out, while the diaphragm flattens and moves down. This increases the volume of the chest cavity. Greater volume means lower pressure inside — so air rushes in from outside. When you breathe out (expiration): both sets of muscles relax. The ribcage drops, the diaphragm domes back up, volume decreases, pressure rises, and air is pushed out.
writeBullets: Inspiration: intercostals contract → ribcage up and out; diaphragm contracts → flattens downward
Inspiration: chest volume increases → pressure falls → air moves in
Expiration: intercostals and diaphragm relax → chest volume decreases → pressure rises → air moves out
Air always moves from high pressure to low pressure

keyTerms.def: Muscles between the ribs that contract during inspiration to raise the ribcage
A dome-shaped sheet of muscle below the lungs; flattens on contraction to increase chest volume
Breathing in — muscles contract, volume increases, pressure decreases
Breathing out — muscles relax, volume decreases, pressure increases

keyPoints.memTip: Think of a bellows: pull the handles apart (inspiration) — volume goes up, pressure goes down, air floods in. Push them together (expiration) — volume goes down, pressure goes up, air comes out. Volume and pressure always go in opposite directions.

keyPoints.heading: Gas exchange — what actually crosses the alveolus wall
keyPoints.content: At the alveolus, two gases are moving at the same time, both by diffusion — from high concentration to low. Oxygen diffuses from the alveolus (where it's concentrated after you inhale) into the blood (where it's been used up by respiration). Carbon dioxide diffuses the other way — from the blood (where it's been produced by respiration) into the alveolus (where its concentration is lower). The blood flow and ventilation (breathing) constantly remove and replenish these gases, keeping the gradients steep so diffusion never slows down.
writeBullets: O₂ diffuses: alveolus → blood (high O₂ in alveolus → low O₂ in blood)
CO₂ diffuses: blood → alveolus (high CO₂ in blood → low CO₂ in alveolus)
Both move by diffusion — no energy required
Ventilation (breathing) maintains the O₂ gradient; blood flow maintains the CO₂ gradient

keyTerms.def: The movement of oxygen into the blood and carbon dioxide out of the blood at the alveoli
The net movement of particles from a region of higher concentration to a region of lower concentration

keyPoints.memTip: O₂ IN, CO₂ OUT — oxygen in (you need it), carbon dioxide out (you don't). The gradient is maintained by two things: breathing (refreshes the air) and blood flow (carries gases away). If either slows, exchange slows — say that in an exam answer and you'll pick up extra marks.

keyPoints.heading: Inhaled vs exhaled air — the numbers
keyPoints.content: The air you breathe in is not the same as the air you breathe out. Inhaled air is roughly 21% oxygen, 0.04% carbon dioxide, and 79% nitrogen. Exhaled air is roughly 16% oxygen, 4% carbon dioxide, and 79% nitrogen — plus it's warmer and carries more water vapour. Oxygen goes down because your cells use it. Carbon dioxide goes up because respiration produces it. Nitrogen doesn't change because your body doesn't use it. Water vapour increases because the moist lining of your airways adds water to the air.
writeBullets: Exhaled: ~16% O₂, ~4% CO₂, ~79% N₂ (plus more water vapour and warmth)
O₂ drops from 21% to 16% — used in respiration
CO₂ rises from 0.04% to 4% — produced in respiration
N₂ stays the same — body does not use it

keyTerms.def: Makes up ~79% of air; not used by the body, so its percentage stays the same in inhaled and exhaled air

keyPoints.memTip: Remember the key numbers: O₂ 21 in → 16 out (loses 5); CO₂ 0.04 in → 4 out (×100 bigger). Nitrogen stays at 79 — it's chemically lazy. Jot these three pairs onto a sticky note on your mirror — you'll have them without trying within a week.

tables.title: Inhaled vs exhaled air
rows: Not used by the body — stays the same
Warmed by body heat in the airways

tables.title: Alveoli adaptations — adaptation and function
rows: More area for diffusion → faster overall gas exchange
Short diffusion distance → faster movement of gases
Gases dissolve in moisture before crossing the membrane
Maintains steep concentration gradients for O₂ and CO₂

1.question: Which feature of alveoli maintains a steep concentration gradient for gas exchange?
1.correctFeedback: Correct — the constant blood flow carries O₂ away and brings CO₂, keeping the gradient steep so diffusion never slows.
1.wrongFeedback: The rich blood supply is the answer. It constantly removes O₂ from the blood side and delivers CO₂ — keeping concentrations different on each side so diffusion keeps happening. The other features help in different ways, but maintaining the gradient is the job of blood flow.

3.question: During inspiration (breathing in), what happens to the diaphragm?
options: It moves up with the ribcage
It stays in the same position
3.correctFeedback: Yes — the diaphragm contracts and flattens downward, increasing the volume of the chest cavity and lowering the pressure so air rushes in.
3.wrongFeedback: During inspiration the diaphragm contracts and flattens downward (option B). This increases chest volume, which lowers pressure inside, so air moves in from outside where the pressure is higher.

commonMistakes: The alveolus wall is ONE CELL thick (not two, not 'very thin' — one cell). The diffusion distance is as short as it can possibly be.
Inhaled air is 21% oxygen and 0.04% CO₂. Exhaled air is 16% oxygen and 4% CO₂. CO₂ increases by a factor of roughly 100, not just a little.
Nitrogen stays at 79% in both inhaled and exhaled air — the body does not use nitrogen, so it doesn't change.
During inspiration, BOTH the intercostal muscles AND the diaphragm contract — not just one of them. Both must be mentioned for full marks.
Gas exchange happens by diffusion only — no energy (ATP) is used to move O₂ or CO₂ across the alveolus wall. Breathing uses energy; the exchange itself does not.
examTips: For 'explain how the alveolus is adapted for gas exchange' questions: name the adaptation, then say what it does. Don't just list features — link each one to its effect on diffusion rate.
If asked about breathing mechanism, make sure you cover BOTH what happens to the ribcage AND what happens to the diaphragm — and always explain the pressure change that follows.
AQA may give you a table of gas percentages and ask you to explain differences. Always link the change to respiration: O₂ decreases because it's used, CO₂ increases because it's produced.

revisionCardBullets: Alveoli: ~300 million tiny air sacs — enormous surface area for gas exchange
Alveolus adaptations: SLIM — Surface area (large), Lining (moist), Inside distance (one cell thick), Maintained gradient (rich blood supply)
O₂ diffuses from alveolus into blood; CO₂ diffuses from blood into alveolus — both by diffusion
Inspiration: intercostals + diaphragm contract → ribcage up/out, diaphragm flattens → volume up → pressure down → air in
Expiration: muscles relax → volume down → pressure up → air out
Exhaled: 16% O₂, 4% CO₂, 79% N₂ (+ more water vapour, warmer)
N₂ stays at 79% — body does not use nitrogen
Blood flow and ventilation maintain steep gradients — if either slows, gas exchange slows
